status_label: Truce Holding — 10% Levy Remains, Section 301 Pending
status_note: The US-China ag tariff truce from the Oct 30, 2025 Busan agreement is holding and was extended through Nov 10, 2026. China lifted its March 2025 retaliatory ag tariffs (up to 15%) on Nov 10, 2025 but kept its broad 10% 'Liberation Day' retaliatory levy, so US soybeans still face roughly 13% (3% MFN + 10%). SCOTUS struck down IEEPA tariffs Feb 20, 2026; the Section 122 global 10-15% surcharge that replaced them (effective Feb 24) was ruled invalid by the Court of International Trade on May 7, 2026 and is under appeal. A May 18, 2026 US-China deal added $17B/yr in US ag purchases through 2028 and resumed beef and poultry access. On June 2, 2026 USTR proposed Section 301 'forced labor' tariffs (10-12.5%, with some agricultural exemptions; hearing July 7) to maintain duties after Section 122 expires around July 24. Verify against USTR and the Federal Register before any trading decision.
headline: US-China ag truce holding post-Busan, but China's retained 10% levy keeps soybeans near 13%; Section 122 surcharge struck by the trade court, Section 301 forced-labor tariffs proposed for July.

rows.note: China lifted its March 2025 retaliatory 10% on soybeans under the Busan agreement (effective Nov 10, 2025) but kept its broad 10% 'Liberation Day' retaliatory levy — so US soybeans face roughly 13% (3% MFN + 10%), a persistent disadvantage vs. Brazil. A Section 301 investigation is pending and could re-escalate.

China suspended its March 2025 retaliatory 15% on corn under the Busan agreement but kept the broad 10% retaliatory levy, leaving roughly 11% (1% MFN + 10%). China purchases remain below pre-trade-war levels; Brazil retains market share.

March 2025 retaliatory 15% suspended under the Busan agreement; the broad 10% retaliatory levy remains (~11% = 1% MFN + 10%). US wheat has modest direct China exposure — global trade flows matter more than this rate.

March 2025 retaliatory 10% suspended under the Busan agreement; the broad 10% retaliatory levy remains (~22% = 12% MFN + 10%). China resumed US beef and poultry access under the May 18, 2026 deal and remains a major US pork market.
USMCA-compliant, duty-free. USMCA joint review underway (US-Mexico bilateral rounds began May 2026). Penn Wharton: USMCA exemption share reached 86.3% of Canada/Mexico imports by February 2026.
USMCA-compliant, duty-free. Mexico remains the top US corn export destination; Canadian demand steady. USMCA joint review underway.

EU anti-dumping duty on US undenatured ethanol in place since 2013 (€83.03/metric ton base); some member states (e.g., Netherlands under RED III) apply higher max rates up to €192/m³. The UK-US trade deal (May 2025) opened a 1.4B L/yr duty-free quota for the UK only. Verify member-state customs for specific shipments.

timeline.event: USTR proposes Section 301 'forced labor' tariffs of 10-12.5% on 59 countries and the EU, with some agricultural exemptions (Annex A). Public comment runs to July 6, hearing July 7 — timed to maintain duties after the Section 122 surcharge expires around July 24.

US-China deal during President Trump's state visit: China to buy at least $17B/yr in US agricultural products through 2028 (in addition to the Oct 2025 soybean commitments) and resumes purchases of US beef and poultry.

US Court of International Trade rules the Section 122 global 10% surcharge (Proclamation 11012) invalid for failing to identify the required balance-of-payments deficit (Oregon v. United States; Burlap & Barrel v. United States). The ruling is under appeal and does not automatically halt collections.

Section 122 global tariff takes effect at 10% (administration announced intent to raise to 15%) after the IEEPA tariffs were terminated. The 150-day statutory clock runs to roughly July 24, 2026 unless Congress extends it.

US Supreme Court rules 6-3 in Learning Resources v. Trump that IEEPA does not authorize tariffs; the April 2025 reciprocal and fentanyl tariffs are voided. The administration pivots to Section 122 the same day.
China lifts the up-to-15% retaliatory ag tariffs from its March 2025 tranche under the Busan agreement, but keeps the broad 10% 'Liberation Day' retaliatory levy — leaving US soybeans at roughly 13%.
Trump-Xi meeting in Busan, South Korea: tariff truce reached. China commits to buy 12 MMT of US soybeans in late 2025 and 25 MMT/yr in 2026-2028; the truce is later extended through Nov 10, 2026.
China imposes first-tranche retaliatory tariffs on US ag (15% on wheat, corn, cotton, chicken; 10% on soybeans, pork, beef, dairy) — later suspended under the Oct 30, 2025 Busan agreement.
